nodes.title: Anderson impurity model: chain representation
nodes.text:
## Anderson impurity model: chain representation

This appendix explains the mapping of the Anderson impurity model (8.8)–(8.11) onto a semi-infinite chain. For simplicity, we may assume that the hybridization parameters $V_{p}$ are real. The goal is to transform the impurity and bath annihilation operators $\{d , c_{p_{1}} , c_{p_{2}} , \ldots \}$ to new operators $\{d , c_{1} , c_{2} , \ldots \}$ such that $H_{0} + H_{\mathrm{bath}} + H_{\mathrm{mix}}$ becomes tridiagonal:

$$
\left( \begin{array} {l l l l l l} {- \mu} & {V_{p_{1}}} & {V_{p_{2}}} & {V_{p_{3}}} & {\dots} \\ {V_{p_{1}}} & {\varepsilon_{p_{1}}} & {} & {} & {} & {} \\ {V_{p_{2}}} & {} & {} & {} & {} & {} \\ {V_{p_{3}}} & {} & {} & {} & {} & {} \\ {} & {} & {} & {} & {} & {\ddots} \end{array} \right) U U^{T} \left( \begin{array} {l l l l l l} {- \mu} & {V_{p_{1}}} & {V_{p_{2}}} & {V_{p_{3}}} & {\dots} \\ {V_{p_{1}}} & {\varepsilon_{p_{1}}} & {} & {} & {} & {} \\ {V_{p_{2}}} & {} & {} & {\varepsilon_{p_{2}}} & {} & {} \\ {V_{p_{3}}} & {} & {} & {} & {\varepsilon_{p_{3}}} & {} \\ {\vdots} & {} & {} & {} & {} & {\ddots} \end{array} \right) U U^{T} \left( \begin{array} {l} {d} \\ {c_{p_{1}}} \\ {c_{p_{2}}} \\ {c_{p_{3}}} \\ {\vdots} \end{array} \right)
$$

$$
= \left( \begin{array} {l l l l l l l} {{d}} & {{c_{1}^{\dagger}}} & {{c_{2}^{\dagger}}} & {{c_{3}^{\dagger}}} & {{\dots}} \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array} {l l l l l l} {{- \mu}} & {{- V}} & {{}} & {{}} & {{}} & {{}} \\ {{- V}} & {{{\tilde{\varepsilon}}_{1}}} & {{- t_{1}}} & {{}} & {{}} & {{}} & {{}} \\ {{}} & {{- t_{1}}} & {{{\tilde{\varepsilon}}_{2}}} & {{- t_{2}}} & {{}} & {{}} & {{}} \\ {{}} & {{}} & {{- t_{2}}} & {{{\tilde{\varepsilon}}_{3}}} & {{\ddots}} \\ {{}} & {{}} & {{}} & {{\ddots}} & {{\ddots}} \end{array} \right) \left( \begin{array} {l} {{d}} \\ {{c_{1}}} \\ {{c_{2}}} \\ {{c_{3}}} \\ {{\vdots}} \end{array} \right) .
$$

The symmetric and orthogonal matrix U associated with this tranformation can be constructed as a product of Householder transformations

$$
h_{\nu} = 1 - \frac{2 \vec{\nu} \vec{\nu}^{T}} {| \vec{\nu} | ^{2}} ,\tag{J.1}
$$

which describe mirror operations. Let us assume that the bath has N sites. In the first step, we choose v such that the vector $\vec{V} = ( V_{p_{1}} \ : V_{p_{2}} \ : V_{p_{3}} \ : \cdot \cdot \cdot V_{p_{N}} )^{T}$ is mapped onto the first element ${\vec{e}}_{1} \colon{\vec{\nu}}_{1} = {\vec{V}} \mp | V | {\vec{e}}_{1} \Rightarrow h_{\nu_{1}} {\vec{V}} = \pm | V | {\vec{e}}_{1}$ , with $\begin{array} {r} {| V | ^{2} = \sum_{p = 1}^{N} | V_{p} | ^{2}} \end{array}$ Defining the block matrix

$$
U_{1} = ( \frac{1} {\mathbf{\sigma}} ) \frac{\mathbf{\sigma}} {h_{\nu_{1}} \mathbf{\sigma}} )\tag{J.2}
$$

with diagonal blocks of dimension 1 and N, respectively, we obtain the transformed Hamiltonian

$$
U_{1} \left( \begin{array} {c} {{\displaystyle - \mu \left| V_{p_{1}} \begin{array} {c l c} {{\displaystyle{V_{p_{1}}}}} & {{\displaystyle{V_{p_{2}}}}} & {{\displaystyle{. . .}}} \\ {{\displaystyle{V_{p_{1}}}}} & {{\displaystyle{\varepsilon_{p_{1}}}}} \end{array} \right.}} \\ {{\displaystyle \quad V_{p_{2}}}} & {{\ddots}} \\ {{\vdots}} \end{array} \right) U_{1} = \left( \begin{array} {c} {{\displaystyle - \mu \left| \pm | V | \begin{array} {c l c} {{\displaystyle 0}} & {{\displaystyle 0}} & {{\displaystyle{. . .}}} \\ {{\displaystyle \pm | V |}} \\ {{\displaystyle 0}} & {{\displaystyle A_{2}}} \\ {{\vdots}} \end{array} \right.}} \\ {{\vdots}} \end{array} \right) .\tag{J.3}
$$

We can now repeat the procedure, and define a transformation $h_{\nu_{2}}$ that maps the vector defined by the off-diagonal elements in the first column of the $N \times N$ matrix $A_{2}$ onto $\vec{e}_{2}$ . The Householder matrix

$$
U_{2} = \left( \frac{1} {0} 1 \right)_{{}} {} ~ \mathrm{~}\tag{J.4}
$$

with diagonal blocks of dimension 2 and $N - 1$ may then be used to bring the Hamiltonian into tridiagonal form up to the second row and column. The transformation U for an impurity model with N bath sites eventually becomes

$$
U = U_{1} U_{2} \cdot \cdot \cdot U_{N - 1} .\tag{J.5}
$$

In the case of multi-orbital systems with diagonal baths, similar transformations map the system onto a star-shaped geometry, with the impurity site at the center.
nodes.summary: 该节点介绍了如何通过Householder变换将Anderson杂质模型的哈密顿量映射到半无限链上的三对角形式，从而实现模型的重构。

nodes.title: Appendix K
nodes.text:
## Appendix K

In this appendix, we derive the effective action for the Anderson impurity model (8.8)–(8.11). We denote the impurity annihilation operators by d, the bath annihilation operators by c, and an arbitrary type of operator by a. The partition function of the impurity model is

$$
Z = \mathrm{Tr}_{d} \mathrm{Tr}_{c} \left[ e^{- \beta H} \right] .
$$

To derive the action, we introduce the coherent states

$$
\vert \xi \rangle = \exp \left[ - \sum_{\alpha} \xi_{\alpha} a_{\alpha}^{\dagger} \right] \vert 0 \rangle ,
$$

which are eigenstates of the destruction operators: $a_{\alpha} | \xi \rangle = \xi_{\alpha} | \xi \rangle$ . The $\xi_{\alpha}$ are elements of a Grassmann algebra, which allows us to account for the anticommutation relations of the Fermionic operators in a path-integral formulation. Specifically, the $\xi_{\alpha}$ satisfy

$$
\{\xi_{\alpha} , \xi_{\beta} \} = \{\xi_{\alpha}^{\ast} , \xi_{\beta} \} = \{\xi_{\alpha}^{\ast} , \xi_{\beta}^{\ast} \} = 0 ,
$$

$$
\{\xi_{\alpha} , a_{\beta} \} = \{\xi_{\alpha} , a_{\beta}^{\dagger} \} = \{\xi_{\alpha}^{*} , a_{\beta} \} = \{\xi_{\alpha}^{*} , a_{\beta}^{\dagger} \} = 0 ,
$$

$$
\partial_{\xi_{\alpha}} \xi_{\alpha} = \int d \xi_{\alpha} \xi_{\alpha} = 1 ,
$$

$$
\partial_{\xi_{\alpha}} 1 = \int d \xi_{\alpha} = 0 .
$$

Three important relations of a Grassman algebra, which we will need in the following, are the scalar product, the closure relation, and the formula for Gaussian integrals:

$$
\langle \xi | \psi \rangle = e^{\sum_{\alpha} \xi_{\alpha}^{*} \psi_{\alpha}} ,\tag{K.1}
$$

$$
\int \prod_{\alpha} d \xi_{\alpha}^{*} d \xi_{\alpha} e^{- \sum_{\alpha} \xi_{\alpha}^{*} \xi_{\alpha}} | \xi \rangle \langle \xi | = 1 ,\tag{K.2}
$$

$$
\int \prod_{\alpha} d \xi_{\alpha}^{*} d \xi_{\alpha} e^{- \sum_{\alpha \beta} \xi_{\alpha}^{*} M_{\alpha \beta} \xi_{\beta} + J_{\alpha}^{*} \xi_{\alpha} + \xi_{\alpha}^{*} J_{\alpha}} = ( \operatorname * {d e t} M ) e^{\sum_{\alpha \beta} J_{\alpha}^{*} [ M^{- 1} ]_{\alpha \beta} J_{\beta}} .\tag{K.3}
$$

Using the coherent states and the anticommutation of Grassmann numbers, we express the partition function as

$$
\begin{array} {l} {{\displaystyle Z = \sum_{n} \langle n | e^{- \beta H} | n \rangle = \int \prod_{\alpha} d \xi_{\alpha}^{*} d \xi_{\alpha} e^{- \sum_{\alpha} \xi_{\alpha}^{*} \xi_{\alpha}} \sum_{n} \langle n | \xi \rangle \langle \xi | e^{- \beta H} | n \rangle}} \\ {{\displaystyle \quad = \int \prod_{\alpha} d \xi_{\alpha}^{*} d \xi_{\alpha} e^{- \sum_{\alpha} \xi_{\alpha}^{*} \xi_{\alpha}} \langle - \xi | e^{- \beta H} | \xi \rangle = \int d \vec{\xi}^{*} d \vec{\xi} e^{- \vec{\xi}^{*} \cdot \vec{\xi}} \langle - \xi | e^{- \beta H} | \xi \rangle ,}} \end{array}
$$

where $\{| n \rangle \}$ is some basis of the impurity model Hilbert space. In the last step, we introduced vectors of Grassmann variables to simplify the notation.

By repeatedly inserting the closure relation (K.2) and introducing antiperiodic boundary conditions, we find

$$
\begin{array} {l} {Z = \displaystyle \int \displaystyle \prod_{m = 0}^{M - 1} d \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} d \vec{\xi}_{m} e^{- \sum_{m = 1}^{M - 1} \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} \cdot \vec{\xi}_{m}} e^{- \vec{\xi}_{0} \vec{\xi}_{0}} \langle - \xi_{0} | e^{- \frac{\beta} {M} H} | \xi_{M - 1} \rangle \cdot \cdot \cdot \langle \xi_{1} | e^{- \frac{\beta} {M} H} | \xi_{0} \rangle} \\ {\approx \displaystyle \int \displaystyle \prod_{m = 1}^{M} d \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} d \vec{\xi}_{m} e^{- \sum_{m = 1}^{M} \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} \cdot \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*}} \displaystyle \prod_{m = 1}^{M} \langle \xi_{m} | 1 - \Delta \tau H ( a^{\dag} , a ) | \xi_{m - 1} \rangle} \\ {= \displaystyle \int \displaystyle \prod_{m = 1}^{M} d \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} d \vec{\xi}_{m} e^{- \sum_{m = 1}^{M} \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} \frac{\vec{\xi}_{m}} {\xi_{m}}} \displaystyle \prod_{m = 1}^{M} ( 1 - \Delta \tau H ( \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} , \vec{\xi}_{m - 1} ) ) \langle \xi_{m} | \xi_{m - 1} \rangle} \\ \approx \displaystyle \int \displaystyle \prod_{m = 1}^{M} d \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} d \vec{\xi}_{m} e^{- \sum_{m = 1}^{M} \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} \frac{\vec{\xi}_{m}} {\xi_{m}}} e^ \sum_{m = 1}^{M} [ - \Delta \tau H ( \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} , \vec{\xi}_ \end{array}
$$

with $\Delta \tau = \beta / M$ . In the limit $\Delta \tau \to 0$ the exponent becomes

$$
\begin{array} {r l r} {{- \sum_{m = 1}^{M} \Delta \tau [ \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} \cdot \frac{\vec{\xi}_{m} - \vec{\xi}_{m - 1}} {\Delta \tau} + H ( \vec{\xi}_{m}^{*} , \vec{\xi}_{m - 1} ) ]}} \\ & {} & {\to - \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau [ \vec{\xi}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \partial_{\tau} \vec{\xi} ( \tau ) + H ( \vec{\xi}^{*} , \vec{\xi} ) ] .} \end{array}
$$

Hence, we obtain the expression for the partition function as a path integral over Grassmann variables:

$$
Z = \int{\cal D} \vec{\xi}^{*} {\cal D} \vec{\xi} e^{- S ( \vec{\xi}^{*} , \vec{\xi} )} ,
$$

$$
S ( \vec{\xi}^{*} , \vec{\xi} ) = \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau \left[ \vec{\xi}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \partial_{\tau} \vec{\xi} ( \tau ) + H ( \vec{\xi}^{*} , \vec{\xi} ) \right] .
$$

To formally define the effective action for the impurity, we distinguish between the Grassmann variables $\xi_{d}$ associated with the impurity and $\xi_{c}$ associated with the bath and write

$$
Z = \int{\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} {\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{d} {\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{c}^{*} {\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{c} e^{- S ( \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} , \vec{\xi}_{d} , \vec{\xi}_{c}^{*} , \vec{\xi}_{c} )} \equiv \int{\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} {\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{d} e^{- S_{\mathrm{eff}} ( \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} , \vec{\xi}_{d} )} .
$$

The Hamiltonian of our impurity model is of the form

$$
H = H_{\mathrm{loc}} + \sum_{p \sigma} \varepsilon_{p} c_{p \sigma}^{\dagger} c_{p \sigma} + \sum_{p \sigma} ( V_{p \sigma} d_{\sigma}^{\dagger} c_{p \sigma} + V_{p \sigma}^{*} c_{p \sigma}^{\dagger} d_{\sigma} ) ,
$$

so the action explicitly reads

$$
\begin{array} {l} {{S = \displaystyle \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau [ \sum_{\sigma} \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \partial_{\tau} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau ) + H_{\mathrm{loc}} ( \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} ( \tau ) , \vec{\xi}_{d} ( \tau ) ) + \sum_{\sigma} ( \vec{\xi}_{, \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \partial_{\tau} \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma} ( \tau )}} \\ {{\displaystyle \qquad + \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \delta \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) + \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \vec{V}_{\sigma} \cdot \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma} ( \tau ) + \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \vec{V}_{\sigma}^{*} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau ) ) ]}} \\ {{\displaystyle = \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau [ \sum_{\sigma} \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \partial_{\tau} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau ) + H_{\mathrm{loc}} ( \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} ( \tau ) , \vec{\xi}_{d} ( \tau ) ) + \sum_{\sigma} ( - \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot M_{\xi e \sigma}^{2} ( \tau )}} \\ {{\displaystyle \qquad + \vec{J}_{\sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma} ( \tau ) + \vec{\xi}_{c \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \cdot \vec{J}_{\sigma} ( \tau ) ) ] ,}} \end{array}
$$

where in the second line we have introduced the matrix $M_{i j} = \delta_{i j} ( - \partial_{\tau} - \varepsilon_{i} )$ and the vectors $\begin{array} {r} {\vec{J}_{\sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) = \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \vec{V}_{\sigma}} \end{array}$ and $\vec{\cal J}_{\sigma} ( \tau ) = \vec{V}_{\sigma}^{*} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau )$

We now use (K.3) to integrate out the bath degrees of freedom:

$$
\begin{array} {l} {{\displaystyle S_{\mathrm{eff}} = \mathrm{const} + \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau \left[ \sum_{\sigma} \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \partial_{\tau} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau ) + H_{\mathrm{loc}} ( \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} ( \tau ) , \vec{\xi}_{d} ( \tau ) ) \right]}} \\ {{\displaystyle \qquad + \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau d \tau^{\prime} \sum_{\sigma} \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \underbrace{\vec{V}_{\sigma} {\cal M}^{- 1} \vec{V}_{\sigma}^{*}}_{\equiv \Delta_{\sigma} ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} )} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} ) .}} \end{array}
$$

The coupling to the bath leads to a retarded term in the effective action, which is given by the hybridization function $\Delta_{\sigma} ( \tau )$ defined in (8.14). In fact, the Matsubara coefficients of this function are

$$
\Delta_{\sigma} ( i \omega_{n} ) = \sum_{p} \frac{| V_{p \sigma} | ^{2}} {i \omega_{n} - \varepsilon_{p}} .
$$

Inserting the definition of $H_{\mathrm{loc}} = U n_{\uparrow} n_{\downarrow} - \mu ( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} )$ , we finally obtain

$$
\begin{array} {l} {{\displaystyle{S_{\mathrm{eff}} = \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau{\cal U} n_{\uparrow} ( \tau ) n_{\downarrow} ( \tau )}}} \\ {{\displaystyle ~ + \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau d \tau^{\prime} \sum_{\sigma} \left[ \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \delta ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} ) \partial_{\tau} \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} ) - \mu \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \delta ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} ) \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} ) \right.}} \\ {{\displaystyle ~ \left. + \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \Delta_{\sigma} ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} ) \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} ) \right]}} \\ {{\displaystyle = \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau{\cal U} n_{\uparrow} ( \tau ) n_{\downarrow} ( \tau ) - \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau d \tau^{\prime} \sum_{\sigma} \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \mathcal{G}_{0 \sigma}^{- 1} ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} ) \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} ) .}} \end{array}
$$

$\mathcal{G}_{0 \sigma}$ is the “Weiss Green’s function” (8.15), which is related to the hybridization function by

$$
\begin{array} {r} {\mathcal{G}_{0 \sigma}^{- 1} ( i \omega_{n} ) = i \omega_{n} + \mu - \Delta_{\sigma} ( i \omega_{n} ) .} \end{array}
$$

The Grassmann path integral expression for the partition function is therefore

$$
Z = \int{\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{d}^{*} {\cal D} \vec{\xi}_{d} e^{- \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau U n_{\uparrow} ( \tau ) n_{\downarrow} ( \tau ) + \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau d \tau^{\prime} \sum_{\sigma} \xi_{d \sigma}^{*} ( \tau ) \mathcal{G}_{0 \sigma}^{- 1} ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} ) \xi_{d \sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} )} .
$$

Alternatively, we can express the partition function in terms of the operators $d , d^{\dag}$ , and the hybridization function $\Delta$ :

$$
\begin{array} {r} {Z = \mathrm{Tr}_{d} \left[ \mathcal{T} e^{- \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau [ U n_{\uparrow} ( \tau ) n_{\downarrow} ( \tau ) - \mu ( n_{\uparrow} ( \tau ) + n_{\downarrow} ( \tau ) ) ] - \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau d \tau^{\prime} \sum_{\sigma} d_{\sigma}^{\dagger} ( \tau ) \Delta_{\sigma} ( \tau - \tau^{\prime} ) d_{\sigma} ( \tau^{\prime} )} \right] .} \end{array}
$$
nodes.summary: 本附录推导了安德森杂质模型的有效作用量，通过引入Grassmann相干态和路径积分表示，将配分函数转化为Grassmann积分形式。

nodes.title: Appendix L Continuous-time auxiliary-field algorithm
nodes.text:
## Appendix L Continuous-time auxiliary-field algorithm

This appendix presents a continuous-time impurity solver that combines the weakcoupling expansion with an auxiliary-field decomposition of the interaction term (Gull et al., 2008). This continuous-time auxiliary-field method (CTAUX) is an adaptation of an algorithm by Rombouts et al. (1999b) for Fermion lattice models. It is suitable for models with density-density interactions and in this case is equivalent to the weak-coupling continuous-time method of Section 8.4.

We present the algorithm for the Anderson impurity model (8.8)–(8.11). Following Rombouts and collaborators, we define $H_{2} = H_{U} - \textstyle{\frac{1} {7}} U ( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} ) - K / \beta$ and $\begin{array} {r} {H_{1} = H - H_{2} = H_{\mu} + \frac{1} {2} U ( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} ) + H_{\mathrm{bath}} + H_{\mathrm{mix}} + K / \beta} \end{array}$ , with K being some arbitrary nonzero constant. After the expansion of the partition function in $H_{2} , ( 8.30 )$ gives the statistical weight of a configuration of n “interaction vertices.” We now extend our configuration space by decoupling each interaction vertex using the discrete Hubbard-Stratonovich decoupling formula (Hirsch, 1983)

$$
- H_{2} = K / \beta - U ( n_{\uparrow} n_{\downarrow} - \frac{1} {2} ( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} ) ) = \frac{K} {2 \beta} \sum_{s = - 1 , 1} e^{\gamma s ( n_{\uparrow} - n_{\downarrow} )} ,
$$

with

$$
\cosh ( \gamma ) = 1 + ( \beta U ) / ( 2 K ) .\tag{L.1}
$$

The configuration space becomes the collection of all possible auxiliary spin configurations $C = \{( \tau_{1} , s_{1} ) , \ldots , ( \tau_{n} , s_{n} ) \} , n = 0 , 1 , \ldots , \tau_{i} \in [ 0 , \beta ) , s_{i} = \pm 1$ on the imaginary-time interval $[ 0 , \beta )$ . These configurations have the weight

$$
w_{C} = \mathrm{Tr} \Big [ e^{- ( \beta - \tau_{n} ) H_{1}} e^{\gamma s_{n} ( n_{\uparrow} - n_{\downarrow} )} \cdots e^{- ( \tau_{2} - \tau_{1} ) H_{1}} e^{\gamma s_{1} ( n_{\uparrow} - n_{\downarrow} )} e^{- \tau_{1} H_{1}} \Big ] \left( \frac{K d \tau} {2 \beta} \right)^{n} .\tag{L.2}
$$

We first separate the electron spin components and then proceed to the analytical calculation of the trace. Introducing $\begin{array} {r} {H_{1}^{\sigma} = - ( \mu - \frac{1} {2} U ) n_{\sigma} + \sum_{p} \varepsilon_{p} c_{p \sigma}^{\dagger} c_{p \sigma} +} \end{array}$ $\begin{array} {r} {\sum_{p} ( V_{p \sigma} d_{\sigma}^{\dagger} c_{p \sigma} + V_{p \sigma}^{*} c_{p \sigma}^{\dagger} d_{\sigma} )} \end{array}$ , we factorize the trace in (L.2) as

$$
\mathrm{Tr} \left[ . . . \right] \propto e^{- K} \prod_{\alpha} \mathrm{Tr} \left[ e^{- ( \beta - \tau_{n} ) H_{1}^{\alpha}} e^{\gamma s_{n} \sigma n_{\sigma}} \cdot \cdot \cdot e^{- ( \tau_{2} - \tau_{1} ) H_{1}^{\sigma}} e^{\gamma s_{1} \sigma n_{\sigma}} e^{- \tau_{1} H_{1}^{\sigma}} \right] .\tag{L.3}
$$

Then, using the identity $e^{\gamma s \sigma n_{\sigma}} = e^{\gamma s \sigma} d_{\sigma}^{\dagger} d_{\sigma} + d_{\sigma} d_{\sigma}^{\dagger} = e^{\gamma s \sigma} - ( e^{\gamma s \sigma} - 1 ) d_{\sigma} d_{\sigma}^{\dagger}$ , we express the trace factors in terms of noninteracting impurity Green’s functions $\tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}$ (with chemical potential shifted as $\mu \to \tilde{\mu} = \mu - {\textstyle \frac{1} {2}} U )$

$$
\mathrm{Tr} \left[ e^{- ( \beta - \tau_{n} ) H_{1}^{\sigma}} e^{\gamma s_{n} \sigma n_{\sigma}} \cdot \cdot \cdot e^{- ( \tau_{2} - \tau_{1} ) H_{1}^{\sigma}} e^{\gamma s_{1} \sigma n_{\sigma}} e^{- \tau_{1} H_{1}^{\sigma}} \right] = \tilde{Z}_{0}^{\sigma} \operatorname*{det} N_{\sigma}^{- 1} ( \{s_{i} , \tau_{i} \} ) ,\tag{L.4}
$$

where $\tilde{Z}_{0}^{\sigma} = \mathrm{Tr} [ e^{- \beta H_{1}^{\sigma}} ]$ . Here, $N_{\sigma}$ represents an $n \times n$ matrix defined by the location of the decoupled interaction vertices, the spin orientations, and the noninteracting Green’s functions:

$$
N_{\sigma}^{- 1} ( \{s_{i} , \tau_{i} \} ) = e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - [ \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ] \Big ( e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - I \Big ) ,
$$

where we used the notations $e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} \equiv \mathrm{diag} ( e^{\gamma \sigma s_{1}} , \ldots , e^{\gamma \sigma s_{n}} ) , [ \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ]_{i j} = \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau_{i} - \tau_{j} )$ for $i \neq j , [ \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ]_{i i} = \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( 0^{+} ) . ^{1}$ Combining (L.2), (L.3), and (L.4) yields

$$
w_{C} = e^{- K} \left( \frac{K d \tau} {2 \beta} \right)^{n} \prod_{\sigma} \tilde{Z}_{0}^{\sigma} \operatorname * {d e t} N_{\sigma}^{- 1} ( \{s_{i} , {\tau}_{i} \} )
$$

for the weight of configuration $C = \{( \tau_{1} , s_{1} ) , . . . , ( \tau_{n} , s_{n} ) \}$

For ergodicity, it is sufficient to insert and remove spins with random orientations at random times. The sampling procedure with fast matrix updates is thus completely analogous to that of the weak-coupling algorithm discussed in Section 8.4.

To compute the contribution of a configuration C to the Green’s function, $G_{C}^{\sigma} ( \tau )$ we insert in (L.2) a creation operator $d_{\sigma}^{\dagger}$ at time 0 and an annihilation operator $d_{\sigma}$ at time τ and divide by $w_{C}$ . Wick’s theorem then leads to

$$
G_{C}^{\sigma} ( \tau ) = \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau ) + \sum_{k = 1}^{n} \sum_{l = 1}^{n} \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau - \tau_{k} ) ( e^{\gamma \sigma s_{k}} - 1 ) [ N_{\sigma}^{( n )} ]_{k l} \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau_{l} ) .
$$

As in the weak-coupling algorithm, it is advantageous to accumulate the quantity

$$
S_{\sigma} ( \tilde{\tau} ) \equiv \sum_{k = 1}^{n} \delta ( \tilde{\tau} - \tau_{k} ) \sum_{l = 1}^{n} \big [ ( e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - I ) N_{\sigma}^{( n )} \big ]_{k l} \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau_{l} ) ,
$$

by binning the time points $\tilde{\tau}$ on a fine grid. Then, after the simulation, the Green’s function is computed from the convolution integral

$$
G^{\sigma} ( \tau ) = \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau ) + \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tilde{\tau} \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau - \tilde{\tau} ) \big \langle S_{\sigma} ( \tilde{\tau} ) \big \rangle_{M C} .
$$

Let us briefly address the role of the parameter K and its effect on the expansion order. It follows from (8.69) that $\begin{array} {r} {\langle - H_{2} \rangle = \frac{1} {\beta} \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau \langle - H ( \tau ) \rangle = \frac{1} {\beta} \langle n \rangle} \end{array}$ , and because $\begin{array} {r} {\langle - H_{2} \rangle = K / \beta - U \langle n_{\uparrow} n_{\downarrow} - \frac{1} {2} ( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} ) \rangle} \end{array}$ , the average perturbation order $\langle n \rangle$ is related to the parameter K and the potential energy $E_{\mathrm{pot}} = U \langle n_{\uparrow} n_{\downarrow} \rangle$ by

$$
\begin{array} {r} {\langle n \rangle = K - \beta E_{\mathrm{pot}} + \frac{1} {2} \beta U \langle ( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} ) \rangle .} \end{array}
$$

Increasing K leads to a higher perturbation order (and thus slower matrix updates), but through (L.1) also to a smaller value of $\gamma$ and thus to less polarization of the auxiliary spins. The average perturbation order grows proportionally to $U$ and the inverse temperature.

For models with density-density interaction, the weak-coupling algorithm discussed in Section 8.4 is equivalent to the continuous-time auxiliary-field method (Mikelsons et al., 2009). The weak-coupling algorithm is based on the following representation of the interaction term (see (8.31)):

$$
\begin{array} {l} {{U n_{\uparrow} n_{\downarrow} = \displaystyle \frac{U} {2} \sum_{s} \left[ n_{\uparrow} - \frac{1} {2} - \left( \frac{1} {2} + \delta \right) s \right] \left[ n_{\downarrow} - \frac{1} {2} + \left( \frac{1} {2} + \delta \right) s \right]}} \\ {{\displaystyle \qquad + \frac{U} {2} \left( n_{\uparrow} + n_{\downarrow} \right) + U \left[ \left( \frac{1} {2} + \delta \right)^{2} - \frac{1} {4} \right] .}} \end{array}
$$

The second term on the right-hand side can be absorbed into a shift of the chemical potential $\mu \tilde{\mu} = \mu - {\textstyle \frac{1} {2}} U$ (with corresponding redefinition of the noninteracting Green’s function $\mathcal{G}_{0} \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}$ and of the noninteracting partition function $Z_{0} \tilde{Z}_{0} )$ , and for future convenience we write the third term as $K^{*} / \beta$ , so that

$$
K^{*} = \beta U \left[ \left( \frac{1} {2} + \delta \right)^{2} - \frac{1} {4} \right] .
$$

With this definition, the weight of a weak-coupling configuration becomes

$$
w_{C}^{\mathrm{w e a k - c o u p l i n g}} ( ( \tau_{1} , s_{1} ) , \dots , ( \tau_{n} , s_{n} ) ) = \tilde{Z}_{0} e^{- K^{*}} \left( - \frac{U d \tau} {2} \right)^{n} \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname * {d e t} {\cal M}_{\sigma}^{- 1} ,
$$

with $[ M_{\sigma}^{- 1} ]_{i j} = \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau_{i} - \tau_{j} ) - \textstyle \frac 12 \delta_{i j} - ( \textstyle \frac 12 + \delta ) s_{i} \sigma \delta_{i j}$ . This expression should be compared with the weight of a corresponding configuration in the CTAUX formalism, which reads

$$
w_{C}^{\mathrm{CTAUX}} ( ( \tau_{1} , s_{1} ) , \dots , ( \tau_{n} , s_{n} ) ) = \tilde{Z}_{0} e^{- K} \left( \frac{K d \tau} {2 \beta} \right)^{n} \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname * {d e t} N_{\sigma}^{- 1} ,
$$

with $[ N_{\sigma}^{- 1} ]_{i j} = [ e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - I ) ]_{i j} = e^{\gamma \sigma s_{i}} \delta_{i j} - \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( \tau_{i} - \tau_{j} ) ( e^{\gamma \sigma s_{j}} - 1 )$ . The parameters $\gamma$ and K are related by cosh $( \gamma ) = 1 + \beta U / 2 K$

To derive the relationship between the weak-coupling parameter δ and the CTAUX parameter $\gamma_{:}$ , which makes the two methods identical, we manipulate the matrix $N_{\sigma}^{- 1}$ with the goal of bringing it into a form analogous to that of $M_{\sigma}^{- 1}$ . The first step is to rewrite

$$
e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} ( e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - I ) = \left( \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} - \frac{1} {2} I - \left\{\frac{1} {2} I - \left[ I - e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} \right]^{- 1} \right\} \right) \left( I - e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} \right) .\tag{L.5}
$$

The expression in the curly brackets is a diagonal matrix with elements $1 / ( 2$ tanh $( \gamma \sigma s / 2 ) ) = \sigma s / ( 2 \operatorname{tanh} ( \gamma / 2 ) )$ . The last identity is true because $\sigma s = \pm 1$ . We furthermore note that $\textstyle \prod_{\sigma} ( I - e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} )$ is a diagonal matrix with elements $2 - 2 \cosh ( \gamma ) =$ $- \beta U / K$ . This means that the last factor in $\left( \mathrm{L} . 5 \right)$ contributes a factor $( - \frac{\beta U} {K} )^{n}$ to the product of determinants. Hence, we obtain

$$
\prod_{\sigma} \operatorname * {d e t} \left( e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} \left( e^{\Gamma_{\sigma}} - I \right) \right) = \left( - \frac{\beta U} {K} \right)^{n} \prod_{\sigma} \operatorname * {d e t} \left( \tilde{\mathcal{G}}_{0}^{\sigma} - \frac{1} {2} I - \frac{1} {2} \frac{1} {\operatorname{tanh} ( \gamma / 2 )} s \sigma I \right) .
$$

By comparing the right-hand side with the expression for $w_{C}^{\mathrm{w e a k - c o u p l i n g}} .$ we conclude that the two algorithms produce configurations $C = \{( \tau_{1} , s_{1} ) , \dots ( \tau_{n} , s_{n} ) \}$ with identical weights if the parameters $\delta$ and $\gamma$ satisfy the condition

$$
\frac{1} {2} + \delta = \frac{1} {2} \frac{1} {\operatorname{tanh} ( \gamma / 2 )} ,
$$

or, equivalently, if ${\cal{K}} = \beta U [ ( \textstyle{\frac{1} {2}} + \delta )^{2} - \textstyle{\frac{1} {4}} ] = {\cal{K}}^{*}$ . Note that $\delta \ge 0$ implies $K \geq 0$
nodes.summary: 此附录介绍了连续时间辅助场方法（CTAUX），一种结合弱耦合展开与Hubbard-Stratonovich变换的杂质求解器，并详细给出了其在安德森杂质模型中的算法实现和配置权重的计算。

nodes.title: Appendix M Continuous-time determinant algorithm
nodes.text:
## Appendix M Continuous-time determinant algorithm

In this appendix, we describe the continuous-time version of the determinant method, which has been developed by Iazzi and Troyer (2014). This algorithm combines the efficient linear-in- $\boldsymbol{\cdot} \beta$ scaling of the determinant algorithm (Chapter 7) with a continuous-time sampling of interaction vertices analogous to the weakcoupling impurity solver described in Section 8.4 of Chapter 8.

First, let us recall that in the discrete-time determinant method we transformed the partition function via a Trotter approximation and Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation into

$$
Z = \sum_{C} \prod_{\sigma} w_{C}^{\sigma} ,
$$

with

$$
w_{C}^{\sigma} = \operatorname*{det} \left[ I + B_{C}^{\sigma} ( \beta , 0 ) \right] .
$$

and

$$
B_{C}^{\sigma} ( \beta , 0 ) \equiv B^{\sigma} ( \beta , \beta - \Delta \tau ) \cdot \cdot \cdot B^{\sigma} ( 2 \Delta \tau , \Delta \tau ) B^{\sigma} ( \Delta \tau , 0 ) ,
$$

a string of multielectron propagators for some noninteracting Hamiltonian. The continuous-time formulation is based on a similar determinant, but avoids a fixed time-discretization and Hubbard-Stratonovich decoupling. Instead the times in the multi-electron propagators are generated by a weak-coupling expansion and are sampled stochastically. We now describe the method for finite-temperature simulations, following the presentation in the original paper (Iazzi and Troyer, 2014).

As in the case of the continuous-time impurity solvers, we switch to an interaction representation. The Hamiltonian of the lattice model is split into $H = H_{0} + H_{1}$ ， where $H_{0}$ is the noninteracting part of the Hamiltonian and $H_{1}$ is the interacting part. In this representation,

$$
H_{1} ( \tau ) = e^{H_{0} \tau} H_{1} e^{- H_{0} \tau} ,
$$

where $H_{1} = \textstyle \sum_{i} V_{i}$ and $V_{i}$ is some site-dependent two-body interaction in a lattice of N sites. To be specific, let us consider the Hubbard model, for which $V_{i} ~ =$ $\begin{array} {r} {U ( n_{i \uparrow} - \frac{1} {2} ) ( n_{i \downarrow} - \frac{1} {2} )} \end{array}$ . For future convenience, we also introduce the dimensionless vertex $\nu_{i} = 4 ( n_{i \uparrow} - \textstyle{\frac{1} {2}} ) ( n_{i \downarrow} - \textstyle{\frac{1} {2}} )$ . As in the case of the weak-coupling continuous-time solver, we now expand the partition function in powers of the interaction term

$$
Z = \sum_{k} \int_{0}^{\beta} d \tau_{1} \cdot \cdot \cdot \int_{\tau_{k - 1}}^{\beta} d \tau_{k} \sum_{i_{1} , \dots , i_{k}} w ( C ) ,
$$

where

$$
w ( C ) = \mathrm{Tr} \left[ e^{- \beta H_{0}} \prod_{\ell = 1}^{k} ( - V_{i_{\ell}} ( \tau_{\ell} ) ) \right] = ( - U / 4 )^{k} \mathrm{Tr} \left[ e^{- \beta H_{0}} \prod_{\ell = 1}^{k} ( \nu_{i_{\ell}} ( \tau_{\ell} ) ) \right]
$$

and $C = \{( i_{1} , \tau_{1} ) , . . . , ( i_{k} , \tau_{k} ) \}$ is a configuration of k interaction vertices at spacetime coordinates $( i_{\ell} , \tau_{\ell} )$

Since the propagation from one vertex to the next is given by the noninteracting part of the Hamiltonian, the weight can (in a manner analogous to the method described in Chapter 7) be expressed as a determinant,

$$
w ( C ) = ( - U / 4 )^{k} \operatorname * {d e t} [ I + B ( C , \beta ) ] , \quad B ( C , \beta ) = e^{- \beta H_{0}} \prod_{\ell = 1}^{k} \nu ( i_{\ell} , \tau_{\ell} ) ,\tag{M.1}
$$

where $\nu ( i_{\ell} , \tau ) = e^{H_{0} \tau} \nu_{i_{\ell}} e^{- H_{0} \tau}$ , and the $2 \times 2$ matrices $H_{0}$ and $\nu_{i_{\ell}} = e^{i \pi ( n_{i_{\ell} \uparrow} + n_{i_{\ell} \downarrow} )}$ have the elements

$$
\begin{array} {l} {{[ H_{0} ]_{i \sigma , j \sigma^{\prime}} = - t_{i j} \delta_{\sigma \sigma^{\prime}} ,}} \\ {{{} [ \nu_{i_{\ell}} ]_{i \sigma , j \sigma^{\prime}} = - 2 \delta_{i , i_{\ell}} \delta_{i_{\ell} , j} \delta_{\sigma \uparrow} \delta_{\sigma^{\prime} \uparrow} - 2 \delta_{i , i_{\ell}} \delta_{i_{\ell} , j} \delta_{\sigma \downarrow} \delta_{\sigma^{\prime} \downarrow} + \delta_{i j} \delta_{\sigma \sigma^{\prime}} .}} \end{array}
$$

The fact that the weights are such determinants allows us to use measurement formulas for Green’s functions and other observables that are similar to those used in the discrete-time determinant methods. The Monte Carlo sampling and updating, however, are analogous to the procedures used in the weak-coupling impurity solver.

The factor $( - U / 4 )^{k}$ in (M.1) introduces a sign problem, which, as in the case of the weak-coupling impurity solver, we can avoid by introducing auxiliary fields that shift the chemical potential for spin up and down or by expanding in a shifted interaction term, as in the CTAUX method (Appendix L). Following Iazzi and Troyer (2014), we choose the latter approach and expand the partition function in powers of $\begin{array} {r} {V_{i} = - U ( 1 - n_{i \uparrow} n_{i \downarrow} ) \equiv - \frac{1} {2} U \nu_{i}} \end{array}$ . Introducing the auxiliary Ising variable α (Appendix G), we then write

$$
\nu_{i} = 2 ( 1 - n_{i \uparrow} n_{i \downarrow} ) = \sum_{\alpha = \pm 1} ( 1 + \alpha n_{i \uparrow} ) ( 1 - \alpha n_{i \downarrow} ) .
$$

The configuration $C = \{( i_{1} , \tau_{1} , \alpha_{1} ) , . . . , ( i_{k} , \tau_{k} , \alpha_{k} ) \}$ becomes a collection of k auxiliary spin variables, and the matrix for the auxiliary spin dependent vertex becomes

$$
[ \nu_{i_{\ell}}^{\alpha_{\ell}} ]_{i \sigma , j \sigma^{\prime}} = \delta_{i j} \delta_{\sigma \sigma^{\prime}} + \alpha_{\ell} \delta_{i , i_{\ell}} \delta_{i_{\ell} , j} \delta_{\sigma \uparrow} \delta_{\sigma^{\prime} \uparrow} - \alpha_{\ell} \delta_{i , i_{\ell}} \delta_{i_{\ell} , j} \delta_{\sigma \downarrow} \delta_{\sigma^{\prime} \downarrow} .
$$

In complete analogy to the sampling discussed in Section 8.4, the Monte Carlo sampling of the configurations C proceeds by random insertion and removal of vertices. For example, if the initial configuration C has k vertices and the new configuration with $k + 1$ vertices is $C^{\prime}$ , then

$$
\mathcal{R} ( C^{\prime} \gets C ) = \frac{\beta N | U |} {k + 1} \frac{\operatorname*{det} [ I + B ( C^{\prime} , \beta ) ]} {\operatorname*{det} [ I + B ( C , \beta ) ]} .
$$

The computational bottleneck is the updating of the matrix $B ( C , \beta )$ . To do this calculation in a time $\mathcal{O} ( \beta N^{3} )$ , Iazzi and Troyer (2014) propose to switch to the eigenbasis of $H_{0} \colon H_{0} = O E O^{\dagger}$ , with E a diagonal matrix and O being an orthonormal transformation. In this basis, the time-evolution operators become diagonal, while the vertex operators become an identity matrix plus an outer product of two vectors: $\delta_{k k^{\prime}} + \gamma O_{k i_{\ell}}^{\dagger} O_{i_{\ell} k^{\prime}}$ . As in the case of the finite-temperature determinant algorithm (Chapter 7), the calculation of the matrix products requires a stabilization procedure.

Equal-time observables are expressible as functions of the matrix B. For example, the equal-time Green’s function $\langle c_{i \sigma} c_{j \sigma^{\prime}}^{\dagger} \rangle$ is obtained by computing the matrix elements of

$$
G = B ( C , \beta ) [ I + B ( C , \beta ) ]^{- 1} .
$$

The time-dependent Green’s function at time $\tau$ is measured using the partial propagator $B ( C , \tau ) ~ = ~ e^{- \tau H_{0}} \prod_{\tau_{i} < \tau} \nu ( i_{\ell} , \tau_{\ell} )$ . In this case, we accumulate the matrix elements of

$$
\begin{array} {r} {G ( \tau ) = B ( C , \tau ) [ I + B ( C , \beta ) ]^{- 1} .} \end{array}
$$

The continuous-time version of the zero-temperature determinant algorithm (Appendix I) has been discussed by Wang et al. (2015).
nodes.summary: 该附录介绍了由Iazzi和Troyer（2014）开发的连续时间行列式算法，它结合了行列式算法的线性β标度和连续时间采样相互作用顶点，避免了离散化的Trotter近似和Hubbard-Stratonovich变换。

nodes.title: Appendix N Correlated sampling
nodes.text:
## Appendix N Correlated sampling

Correlated sampling is a technique for reducing variance. It is sometimes called differential Monte Carlo because its principal use is for reducing the variance of the average difference between two quantities. A related use is estimating derivatives. The method exploits the fact that by promoting a statistical dependence between variables, often simply by having estimates share all or part of a sequence of random numbers, the total variance is less than the sum of the variances.

To explain the basic idea, suppose we seek a Monte Carlo estimate of the integral

$$
{\cal F} = \int d x f ( x ) p_{X} ( x ) ,
$$

where $p_{X} ( x )$ is the probability distribution of a random variable X. In other words, $F = \langle f ( x ) \rangle$ . We now consider another random variable Y, which is not necessarily independent of X, and construct a third random variable

$$
\xi = f ( X ) - g ( Y ) ,
$$

where for the time being $g ( Y )$ is arbitrary. If $G = \langle g ( y ) \rangle$ , then we can express the variance of $\xi$ as

$$
\operatorname{var} ( \xi ) = \operatorname{var} ( f ) + \operatorname{var} ( g ) - 2 \rho{\sqrt{\operatorname{var} \left( f \right) \operatorname{var} \left( g \right)}} ,\tag{N.1}
$$

where $\rho$ is the correlation coefficient

$$
\rho = \frac{\langle ( f - F ) ( g - G ) \rangle} {\sqrt{\operatorname{var} ( f ) \operatorname{var} ( g )}} .
$$

As noted in Section $3.6 , - 1 \leq \rho \leq 1$ , and if X and Y are statistically independent, that is, uncorrelated, $\rho = 0$ and the variances add.

If G is known, then since

$$
F = \langle \xi \rangle + G ,
$$

we could estimate F by estimating ξ. From (N.1), we see that the variance of $\xi$ is small if $\rho$ is close to unity. In fact, if $X = Y$ and $g = f$ , that is, the expectation values are perfectly correlated, then $\rho ~ = ~ 1$ and the variance of $\zeta$ equals zero. Consequently, when $X = Y$ and $g \approx f$ , it is more advantageous to sample $\xi$ than $X .$

We comment that correlated sampling is often used in the following way: Suppose the random variable Y is correlated with the random variable X and we can compute cov(X, Y) (3.23). If we want to estimate X, know Y, and compute $\operatorname{var} ( Y )$ , then we construct the random variable $X ( a ) = X + a ( Y - \langle Y \rangle )$ . Noting that X(a) and X have the same mean, we write

$$
\operatorname{var} ( X ( a ) ) = \operatorname{var} ( X ) - 2 a \operatorname{cov} ( X , Y ) + a^{2} \operatorname{var} ( Y ) .
$$

If we take $a = \operatorname{cov} ( X , Y ) / \operatorname{var} ( Y )$ , then

$$
\operatorname{var} ( X ( a ) ) = \left[ 1 - \rho_{X Y}^{2} \right] \operatorname{var} ( X ) < \operatorname{var} ( X ) .
$$

Finally, we note that if $\left. X \right. = \left. Y \right.$ , then we can always choose an a such that the variance of $X ( a ) = a X + ( 1 - a ) Y$ is smaller that the variance of X.

In both the energy and energy-variance minimization variational Monte Carlo methods (Section 9.3), the basic step is generating a sequence of trial states $| \psi_{T}^{1} \rangle , \psi_{T}^{2} \rangle , \dots , | \psi_{T}^{i} \rangle , . . .$ . from a base state $| \psi_{T}^{0} \rangle$ , where the parameters of each $| \psi_{T}^{i} \rangle$ differ only slightly from those of $| \psi_{T}^{0} \rangle$ . If we were to compute the energy of each state by drawing independent configurations, the statistical errors of the differences between any pair of estimates would add and their confidence intervals could overlap. By drawing configurations from the base trial state and computing all energies with this common set of configurations, the statistical error of the difference between any pair is less than the sum because the pair’s estimate is correlated.
nodes.summary: 相关抽样是一种利用随机变量之间的统计相关性来降低方差的蒙特卡洛技术，常用于估计两个量的差异或导数，并通过数学公式展示了如何通过调整相关系数来最小化方差。

nodes.title: Appendix O The Bryan algorithm
nodes.text:
## Appendix O The Bryan algorithm

There are multiple ways to maximize a nonlinear function of N variables. Here we describe the Levenberg-Marquardt method (Golub and Loan, 1989; Press et al., 2007), which is a variation of Newton’s method. After its description, we discuss how to adjust it for the specific problem of maximizing Q as stated in (12.39).

Our problem here is to maximize some general function

$$
Q ( A ) = Q ( A_{1} , A_{2} , . . . , A_{N} )
$$

with respect to A. Starting from some initial guess for A, we iteratively improve it until the procedure converges. Suppose our current guess is A and we want to find a better solution A-. We start by considering the Taylor series expansion of Q about some point A,

$$
\begin{array} {r} {\boldsymbol{Q} \left( \boldsymbol{A}^{\prime} \right) \approx \boldsymbol{Q} \left( \boldsymbol{A} \right) + \boldsymbol{\delta A}^{T} \cdot \boldsymbol{g} - \frac{1} {2} \boldsymbol{\delta A}^{T} \cdot \boldsymbol{H} \cdot \boldsymbol{\delta A} ,} \end{array}\tag{O.1}
$$

where the vector δA is the deviation $\delta A = A^{\prime} - A$

$$
g_{i} = \left. \frac{\partial Q} {\partial A_{i}} \right| _{A}\tag{O.2}
$$

are the components of the gradient of Q evaluated at A, and

$$
H_{i j} = - \frac{\partial^{2} Q} {\partial A_{i} \partial A_{j}} \bigg | _{A}\tag{O.3}
$$

are the elements of the Hessian matrix of $Q$ evaluated at A. Near the maximum, the Hessian is positive definite.

We can regard (O.1) as defining a local approximation for the gradient

$$
\nabla Q \approx g - H \cdot \delta A .
$$

Because we want to make the gradient at $A^{\prime}$ vanish, within the second-order approximation (O.1), we should make δA satisfy

$$
g \approx H \cdot \delta A ,
$$

so the relation between $A^{\prime}$ and A is

$$
\begin{array} {r} {A^{\prime} \approx A + H^{- 1} \cdot g .} \end{array}
$$

If the function $Q$ were in fact quadratic with a positive-definite Hessian, then the above analysis shows that we could move from $A$ to the maximum $\hat{\boldsymbol A}$ in a single step, that is, $\hat{A} = A + H^{- 1} g$ . When (O.1) is not exact, we can execute this stepping procedure iteratively,

$$
\begin{array} {c} {{( H + \mu I ) \cdot \delta A = g ,}} \\ {{A^{\prime} A + \delta A ,}} \end{array}\tag{O.4}
$$

until convergence (as measured, for example, by $0 < \sqrt{g^{T} \cdot g} \ll 1 )$ . The method will converge provided we can keep stepping uphill. Adding the matrix $\mu I$ to the Hessian shifts its eigenvalues, and adjusting $\mu$ at each step to maintain the local positive definiteness of $\mu I + H$ keeps us stepping uphill. This is the Levenberg-Marquardt strategy, summarized in Algorithm 51.

In Algorithm 51, what do we mean by solving for δA? Clearly, $\left[ \mu I + H \right] \cdot \delta A = g$ is a simultaneous system of linear equations for δA. For a given value of $\mu$ , we use the standard Gaussian elimination method with partial pivoting to solve them for δA, and check whether the new position $A + \delta A$ moves the new value of $Q$ above the old. If not, we could then repeat the process with new values of $\mu$ until it does. When we finally step uphill, we accept the new position and continue iterating.

```perl
Algorithm 51 Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm.
Input: Starting point A and a convergence parameter  .
Set ν = 10, μ- = 0.01, A- = A, and Q- = Q(A) ;
repeat
$\mu \mu^{\prime} / \nu$ ;
repeat
Solve [μI + H(A-)] · δA = g(A-) for δA ;
if Q(A + δA) < Q- then
μ ← μν ;
end if
until Q(A + δA) > Q-.
μ- ← μ ;
A- ← A + δA ;
Q- ← Q(A + δA) ;
until $\left\| g ( A^{\prime} ) \right\| _{2} < \epsilon .$
return $A^{\prime} .$
```

Because H is symmetric, we alternatively could just as easily diagonalize it, writing the diagonalization as $H = Z \cdot \Xi \cdot Z^{T}$ , where $Z$ is an orthonormal matrix and $\Xi$ is a diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are the eigenvalues $\xi_{i}$ of H. We now transform the system of linear equations

$$
( H + \mu I ) \cdot \delta A = g \to ( \mu + \xi_{i} ) \delta A_{i}^{\prime} = g_{i}^{\prime}
$$

and thereby transform the coupled system of linear equations into a set of independent linear equations. Ensuring we step uphill becomes equivalent to adjusting μ so that $\mu + \xi_{i} > 0$ for all i. Because an orthonormal transformation preserves norms, our stopping criterion

$$
\| g \| _{2} = \sqrt{g^{T} \cdot g} = \sqrt{[ g^{\prime} ]^{T} \cdot g^{\prime}} < \epsilon
$$

remains unaltered by the coordinate transformation.

For our specific problem discussed in Section 12.3, the gradient is

$$
\nabla Q = \alpha \nabla S - \nabla{\mathcal L} ,
$$

with

$$
\nabla S = - \ln ( A_{i} / m_{i} ) , \quad \nabla{\mathcal{L}} = K^{T} \cdot C^{- 1} \cdot \delta G ,
$$

where $\delta G$ is a vector whose components are $G_{i} - \bar{G}_{i}$ . The gradient of S follows from (12.43).

When $\nabla Q = 0$

$$
\alpha \ln{( A_{i} / m_{i} )} = - K^{T} \cdot \left[ C^{- 1} \cdot \delta G \right] .
$$

The expression $C^{- 1}$ · δG is just a vector. What the above says is that the solution is a linear combination of the vectors representing the columns of $K^{T}$ . With the singular value decomposition $K^{T} = U \cdot D \cdot V^{T}$ , these vectors are actually the orthogonal columns of $U .$ Based on this observation, Bryan (1990) suggested parameterizing the solution as

$$
A_{i} = m_{i} \exp \bigg ( \sum_{j} U_{i j} u_{j} \bigg ) .\tag{O.5}
$$

The condition for the maximum then becomes

$$
\alpha u = - D \cdot V^{T} \cdot C^{- 1} \cdot \delta G .
$$

Hence,

$$
g = \alpha u + D \cdot V^{T} \cdot C^{- 1} \cdot \delta G
$$

is the gradient in the space defined by the parameters u. The Hessian in this space is

$$
\boldsymbol{H} = \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{g}} {\partial \boldsymbol{u}} = \alpha \boldsymbol{I} + \boldsymbol{D} \cdot \boldsymbol{V}^{T} \cdot \boldsymbol{C}^{- 1} \cdot \boldsymbol{K} \cdot \frac{\partial \boldsymbol{A}} {\partial \boldsymbol{u}} .
$$

We now proceed to the maximization procedure, invoking $H \delta u = g$ for the Newton step using the just-defined expressions for g and H.

At each step of the iteration, we must compute g and H for the current estimate of u. We now recall, however, that before the start of the maximization procedure, we transformed the Green’s function and the kernel into a space where the covariance matrix C is diagonal, so $C^{- 1}$ is some diagonal matrix  (12.37). We also have $K = V \cdot D \cdot U^{T}$ . Accordingly,

$$
H = \frac{\partial g} {\partial u} = \alpha I + \left[ D \cdot V^{T} \cdot \Sigma \cdot V \cdot D \right] \cdot U^{T} \cdot \frac{\partial A} {\partial u} .
$$

We note that

$$
\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{A}} {\partial \boldsymbol{u}} = \boldsymbol{\mathcal{A}} \cdot \boldsymbol{U} ,
$$

where is a diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are $A_{i}$ . Finally, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} {r} {H = \alpha I + \left[ D \cdot V^{T} \cdot \Sigma \cdot V \cdot D \right] \cdot \left[ U^{T} \cdot \mathcal{A} \cdot \mathcal{U} \right] \equiv \alpha I + X \cdot Y .} \end{array}
$$

When we restrict the search space to the s-dimensional sub-dominant space of the kernel K, each of these matrices and their product becomes a symmetric $s \times s$ matrix. The solution to this reduced system of linear equations becomes a numerical task that we could handle by Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting. We could also proceed as before by diagonalizing the symmetric matrix $X \cdot Y$ . However, another, more preferable strategy is available.

The preferred strategy transforms H into a form similar to one we would get with the diagonalization. First, we note that X depends on the matrices we obtain from singular-value decomposition of the kernel K and the diagonalization of the covariance C. Because these matrix factorizations are done prior to starting the maximization procedure, they are done only once, and accordingly X is invariant during the maximization procedure. On the other hand, Y changes at each step as the values of $u_{i}$ and hence $A_{i}$ change. We also note that the matrix U, completing the definition of Y, does not change.

To transform H, we first diagonalize Y and write $\boldsymbol{Y} = \boldsymbol{W} \cdot \boldsymbol{\Omega} \cdot \boldsymbol{W}^{T}$ , where W is an orthonormal matrix and  is a diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are the eigenvalues of Y. Next, we define the symmetric matrix $P = \sqrt{\Omega} \cdot W \cdot X \cdot W^{T} \cdot \sqrt{\Omega}$ and then diagonalize it, writing ${\cal P} = {\cal Z} \cdot \Xi \cdot{\cal Z}^{T}$ . If we define $F = W \cdot \sqrt{\Omega^{- 1}} \cdot Z ,$ then direct substitution shows that $F^{- T} \cdot F^{- 1} = Y$ . Now, we perform the series of transformations

$$
\begin{array} {r} {\begin{array} {c} {[ \alpha I + X \cdot Y ] \cdot \delta u = g \to} \\ {{\left[ \left( \alpha + \mu \right) I + \Xi \right] ( F \cdot \delta u ) = F \cdot g \to}} \\ {{\left[ \left( \alpha + \mu \right) I + \Xi \right] \delta u^{\prime} = g^{\prime}}} \end{array}} \end{array}\tag{O.6}
$$

and again reduce the simultaneous system of linear equations to a set of independent linear equations but in this case for the step $\delta u^{\prime} .$ . The Levenberg-Marquardt phase of the procedure chooses $\mu$ such that $\alpha + \xi_{i} + \mu > 0$ for all i.

The transformation just made is not norm preserving. The stopping condition $\sqrt{[ \delta u^{\prime} ]^{T} \cdot \delta u^{\prime}} < \epsilon$ is, however, equivalent to $\sqrt{\delta u^{T} \cdot Y \cdot \delta u} < \epsilon$ . Before parametrizing A by u and being mindful of the metric for flat curvature of the entropy, we would have used the stopping criterion $\sqrt{\sum_{i} ( \delta A_{i} )^{2} / A_{i}} ~ < ~ \epsilon$ . Remarkably, $\begin{array} {r} {\sum_{i} ( \delta A_{i} )^{2} / A_{i} = \sum_{i j} \delta u_{i} Y_{i j} \delta u_{j}} \end{array}$ ; that is, Y is the metric in u-space.

The readily available LAPACK and BLAS software facilitates the numerical implementation of this algorithm, which is due to Bryan (1990).
nodes.summary: 该节点描述了Levenberg-Marquardt方法（一种牛顿法的变体）用于最大化多元非线性函数，包括其迭代更新公式和通过调整参数μ保持上坡搜索的策略。

nodes.summary:
acceptance probability: 接受概率，在Metropolis等算法中决定是否接受新状态的概率。
algorithm pseudocode analytic continuation: 解析延拓算法的伪代码描述。
comb reconfiguration: 梳状重配置方法，用于更新权重或样本配置。
constrained-path method: 约束路径方法，用于量子蒙特卡洛中抑制符号问题。
B propagation: B传播算法，可能是量子蒙特卡洛中的传播步骤。
continuous-time cluster algorithm: 连续时间团簇算法，用于模拟量子自旋系统。
directed loop algorithm: 有向环算法，用于高效更新量子蒙特卡洛中的环配置。
“on the fly,”: 实时生成算法，在运行时动态计算所需量。
“on-the-fly” S = 1/2 XY model: 实时生成的S=1/2 XY模型算法。
discrete probability sampling: 离散概率采样方法。
elegant: 优雅的采样方法。
standard: 标准离散概率采样。
DMFT self-consistency loop: 动力学平均场自洽循环，用于求解局域自能。
finite T determinant method: 有限温度行列式蒙特卡洛方法。
heat-bath algorithm: 热浴算法，一种马尔可夫链蒙特卡洛更新规则。
LDA+DMFT self-consistency loop: LDA+DMFT联合自洽循环。
Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm: 莱文贝格-马夸尔特算法，用于非线性最小二乘拟合。
long-range interactions: 长程相互作用处理方法。
loop algorithm directed: 有向环算法。
quantum: 量子环算法。
Metropolis algorithm: 梅特罗波利斯算法，经典蒙特卡洛方法。
single-spin flip: 单自旋翻转更新。
modified Gram-Schmidt method: 改进的格拉姆-施密特正交化方法。
column orthonormalization: 列正交归一化。
UDV factorization: UDV分解，矩阵分解的一种。
Monte Carlo program structure: 蒙特卡洛程序整体结构。
Newton’s method: 牛顿法，用于求根或优化。
power method Monte Carlo: 幂法蒙特卡洛，用于求矩阵主特征值。
shifted: 平移幂法。
shifted inverse: 平移逆幂法。
quantum loop and cluster algorithm: 量子环和团簇算法。
rejection method: 拒绝采样法。
sampling time-ordered sequence of imaginary times: 虚时时间有序序列采样。
faster: 更快的采样方法。
simple: 简单采样方法。
single-spin update: 单自旋更新。
distributed-memory computer: 分布式内存计算机上的单自旋更新。
shared-memory computer: 共享内存计算机上的单自旋更新。
stochastic series expansion method: 随机级数展开法，一种量子蒙特卡洛方法。
loops and clusters: 随机级数展开中的环和团簇表示。
Swendsen-Wang algorithm: 斯文森-王算法，团簇更新算法。
union-find algorithm: 并查集算法。
serial: 串行并查集。
serial (improved): 改进的串行并查集。
shared-memory computer: 共享内存计算机上的并查集。
wave function optimization methods: 波函数优化方法。
linearized: 线性化波函数优化。
Newton: 牛顿法波函数优化。
weight window reconfiguration: 权重窗口重配置，用于改善采样效率。
Wolff algorithm: 沃尔夫算法，单团簇更新算法。
world-line method: 世界线方法，量子蒙特卡洛之一。
worm algorithm: 虫洞算法，用于计算关联函数。
simplified: 简化虫洞算法。
alias method: 别名方法，从离散分布高效采样。
Anderson impurity model: 安德森杂质模型，量子杂质模型之一。
action: 安德森杂质模型的作用量。
chain representation: 链表示法用于安德森杂质模型。
Anderson lattice model: 安德森晶格模型。
antiferromagnetic structure factor: 反铁磁结构因子。
area law: 面积律，量子纠缠熵与边界面积成正比。
autocorrelation time: 自相关时间，衡量蒙特卡洛样本之间相关性的指标。
auxiliary field method: 辅助场方法，用于相互作用费米子模拟。
BSS algorithm (determinant algorithm): BSS行列式算法，连续时间辅助场方法之一。
continuous-time auxiliary field algorithm: 连续时间辅助场算法。
continuous-time determinant algorithm: 连续时间行列式算法。
Hirsch transformation: 赫希变换，将相互作用项转化为辅助场耦合。
Hirsch-Fye algorithm: 赫希-法伊算法，离散时间辅助场方法。
Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation: 哈伯德-斯特拉托诺维奇变换。
auxiliary fields continuous: 连续辅助场。
auxiliary fields discrete: 离散辅助场。
average spectrum method: 平均谱方法，用于解析延拓。
back propagation: 反向传播，优化神经网络中的参数更新。
bath Green’s function: 浴格林函数，杂质模型中的环境格林函数。
Kondo model: 近藤模型，描述磁性杂质与导体耦合。
Bayesian constrained fit: 贝叶斯约束拟合，利用先验信息拟合数据。
Bayesian statistical inference: 贝叶斯统计推断，基于后验概率进行推理。
Bryan’s method: 布赖恩方法，用于最大熵解析延拓。
constrained fit: 约束拟合，对拟合参数施加约束。
default model: 默认模型，贝叶斯分析中使用的先验分布。
dominant subspace: 主导子空间，用于降维或特殊计算。
evidence: 证据，边际似然，用于模型比较。
historic maximum entropy method: 历史最大熵方法，解析延拓的经典方法。
Jeffery’s prior: 杰弗里斯先验，无信息先验的一种。
likelihood function: 似然函数，给定参数下数据出现的概率。
Ockham factor: 奥卡姆因子，用于模型复杂度惩罚。
posterior probability: 后验概率，观测数据后参数的概率。
prior probability: 先验概率，观测前对参数的信念。
Bayes’s theorem: 贝叶斯定理，后验与先验和似然的关系。
Binder ratio: 宾德比，用于确定相变临界点的累积量。
binomial distribution: 二项分布，描述成功次数的离散分布。
bipartite lattice: 二分格点，可分成两套子格点的晶格。
block inversion: 块矩阵求逆，用于求解大规模线性系统。
blocking analysis: 块分析，通过合并样本块估计误差。
blocking averages: 块平均，用于自相关时间估计。
bond dimension: 键维数，张量网络中的关键参数。
bootstrap analysis: 自举分析，重采样方法用于误差估计。
Bose-Hubbard model: 玻色-哈伯德模型，描述玻色子在格点上的相互作用。
Box-Muller method: 博克斯-穆勒方法，生成正态分布随机数。
central limit theorem: 中心极限定理，解释样本均值分布趋近正态。
Chapman-Kolmogoroff equation: 查普曼-科尔莫戈罗夫方程，马尔可夫链转移概率满足的关系。
checkerboard decomposition: 棋盘分解，用于并行更新量子蒙特卡洛。
checkerboard representation: 棋盘表示，将格点分为奇偶子格。
classic maximum entropy method: 经典最大熵方法，一种解析延拓技术。
classicization: 经典化，将量子问题转化为经典模拟。
closed-shell effects: 闭壳效应，原子或分子中满壳层的影响。
cluster algorithm: 团簇算法，通过集体更新加速蒙特卡洛。
cluster DMFT: 团簇动力学平均场，考虑空间关联的扩展DMFT。
coherent states: 相干态，量子光学常用态。
conditional mean: 条件均值，给定条件下的期望值。
conditional probability chain rule: 概率链式法则，分解联合概率。
conditional variance: 条件方差，给定条件下的方差。
confidence in: 置信度，统计推断中对结果的信任程度。
